title: Osimertinib in Untreated EGFR-Mutated Advanced Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA)
text_chunk: The FLAURA trial was a randomized, double-blind, phase 3 study comparing osimertinib, a third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor, with standard EGFR-TKIs (gefitinib or erlotinib) as first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated advanced NSCLC. A total of 556 patients with EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutations were enrolled. The primary endpoint was investigator-assessed progression-free survival. Osimertinib demonstrated a significantly longer median PFS of 18.9 months compared with 10.2 months for the comparator TKIs (hazard ratio 0.46, 95% CI 0.37-0.57, p<0.001). Notably, osimertinib showed superior CNS efficacy with a CNS PFS HR of 0.48. The overall survival analysis published subsequently showed a median OS of 38.6 months for osimertinib versus 31.8 months for comparator TKIs (HR 0.80, p=0.046). The safety profile of osimertinib was favorable, with lower rates of grade 3 or higher adverse events (34% vs 45%). This trial established osimertinib as the preferred first-line EGFR TKI and fundamentally changed the treatment paradigm for EGFR-mutant NSCLC by demonstrating that starting with a third-generation agent improves both PFS and OS compared with sequential therapy.

title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan versus Trastuzumab Emtansine for Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast03)
text_chunk: DESTINY-Breast03 was a randomized, open-label, phase 3 trial comparing trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) with trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1) in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer previously treated with trastuzumab and a taxane. The trial enrolled 524 patients. T-DXd demonstrated a dramatic improvement in median PFS of 28.8 months versus 6.8 months for T-DM1 (HR 0.33, p<0.001). The ORR was 79% for T-DXd versus 35% for T-DM1. Overall survival at 24 months favored T-DXd (77.4% vs 69.9%). Interstitial lung disease (ILD) occurred in 15.2% of T-DXd patients (grade 5 in 0.8%), necessitating proactive monitoring. The bystander killing effect of the topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd) enables activity even in HER2-heterogeneous tumors. DESTINY-Breast03 established T-DXd as the undisputed standard second-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, displacing T-DM1 which had been the standard for nearly a decade.

title: Pembrolizumab versus Chemotherapy for PD-L1-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (KEYNOTE-024)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-024 was a pivotal phase 3, open-label, randomized trial comparing pembrolizumab monotherapy with investigator's choice platinum-based chemotherapy in patients with previously untreated advanced NSCLC with PD-L1 tumor proportion score (TPS) of 50% or greater and no EGFR or ALK alterations. The trial enrolled 305 patients. Pembrolizumab demonstrated significantly improved median PFS of 10.3 months versus 6.0 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.50, p<0.001) and median OS of 26.3 months versus 14.2 months (HR 0.62). ORR was 44.8% vs 27.8%. The trial established immunotherapy monotherapy as the preferred first-line treatment for PD-L1-high NSCLC, fundamentally changing the treatment algorithm. Updated 5-year analysis showed OS rate of 31.9% for pembrolizumab versus 16.3% for chemotherapy, demonstrating durable long-term survival benefit. The safety profile favored pembrolizumab with fewer grade 3-5 adverse events (26.6% vs 53.3%).

title: Pembrolizumab in Microsatellite-Instability-High Advanced Colorectal Cancer (KEYNOTE-177)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-177 was a phase 3, open-label, randomized trial comparing first-line pembrolizumab monotherapy with investigator's choice chemotherapy (mFOLFOX6 or FOLFIRI with or without bevacizumab or cetuximab) in patients with microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) metastatic colorectal cancer. The trial enrolled 307 patients. Pembrolizumab significantly improved median PFS to 16.5 months versus 8.2 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.60, p=0.0002). The ORR was 45% with a CR rate of 13% for pembrolizumab. Importantly, the responses were highly durable with 83% of responders maintaining response at 24 months. KEYNOTE-177 established single-agent pembrolizumab as the new standard first-line treatment for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC, replacing decades of chemotherapy-first approaches for this molecular subgroup. The trial reinforced MSI/MMR testing as a mandatory biomarker in metastatic CRC workup.

title: Olaparib Monotherapy in Patients with Advanced Cancer and a Germline BRCA Mutation (OlympiAD)
text_chunk: OlympiAD was a phase 3, open-label, randomized trial comparing olaparib monotherapy with physician's choice single-agent chemotherapy (capecitabine, eribulin, or vinorelbine) in patients with HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer and a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation who had received no more than two prior chemotherapy lines. The trial enrolled 302 patients. Olaparib demonstrated significantly improved median PFS of 7.0 months versus 4.2 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.58, p<0.001). ORR was 59.8% versus 28.8%. Quality of life assessments favored olaparib with lower rates of grade 3+ adverse events. Final OS analysis showed numerical improvement (19.3 vs 17.1 months) that did not reach statistical significance, likely confounded by extensive crossover. OlympiAD led to FDA approval of olaparib for germline BRCA-mutant HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer and established PARP inhibitors as a precision medicine paradigm in breast cancer.

title: Sotorasib for KRAS p.G12C-Mutated Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (CodeBreaK 100)
text_chunk: CodeBreaK 100 was a landmark phase 1/2 study of sotorasib (AMG 510) in patients with KRAS G12C-mutant advanced solid tumors. In the registrational phase 2 NSCLC cohort of 126 patients, sotorasib demonstrated ORR of 37.1%, disease control rate of 80.6%, and median PFS of 6.8 months. Median duration of response was 11.1 months. The drug was well tolerated with grade 3+ adverse events in 21% of patients, predominantly diarrhea and hepatotoxicity. CodeBreaK 100 was a historic milestone as the first clinical evidence that KRAS, considered undruggable for four decades, could be therapeutically targeted. KRAS G12C is found in approximately 13% of NSCLC. The covalent inhibitor traps KRAS in its inactive GDP-bound state. Responses were observed across co-mutation profiles including STK11, KEAP1, and TP53, though co-mutations in STK11 and KEAP1 were associated with shorter PFS.

title: Lorlatinib in Previously Untreated ALK-Positive Lung Cancer (CROWN)
text_chunk: The CROWN trial was a phase 3, randomized, open-label study comparing lorlatinib, a third-generation ALK inhibitor, with crizotinib as first-line treatment for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. A total of 296 patients were enrolled. Lorlatinib demonstrated a dramatically superior 3-year PFS rate of 64% versus 19% for crizotinib, with median PFS not reached for lorlatinib at 36 months compared to 9.3 months for crizotinib (HR 0.27). The intracranial ORR was 82% versus 23% for crizotinib, with 71% achieving intracranial CR. The 5-year update showed sustained PFS benefit with 60% of lorlatinib patients remaining progression-free. Key adverse effects included hypercholesterolemia (81%), hypertriglyceridemia (67%), weight gain, edema, and CNS effects (cognitive and mood changes). CROWN established lorlatinib as a compelling first-line option that may prevent or significantly delay CNS metastases and systemic resistance in ALK-positive NSCLC.

title: Combined Nivolumab and Ipilimumab versus Ipilimumab Alone in Previously Untreated Advanced Melanoma (CheckMate-067)
text_chunk: CheckMate-067 was a landmark phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing nivolumab plus ipilimumab combination, nivolumab monotherapy, and ipilimumab monotherapy in 945 treatment-naive patients with unresectable stage III or IV melanoma. At the 6.5-year follow-up, median OS was 72.1 months for nivolumab plus ipilimumab, 36.9 months for nivolumab, and 19.9 months for ipilimumab. The 6.5-year OS rates were 49%, 42%, and 23%. Nivolumab plus ipilimumab achieved the highest ORR of 58% with a CR rate of 22%. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 59% of the combination group but were generally manageable with corticosteroid-based algorithms. BRAF mutation status did not significantly affect the benefit from immunotherapy. CheckMate-067 established dual checkpoint blockade as the reference regimen for advanced melanoma with the longest median OS reported in a randomized trial and informed the development of combination immunotherapy across multiple cancer types.

title: Efficacy of Selpercatinib in RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (LIBRETTO-001)
text_chunk: LIBRETTO-001 was a phase 1/2, multicenter, open-label study of selpercatinib in patients with RET-altered solid tumors. In treatment-naive RET fusion-positive NSCLC (n=69), selpercatinib demonstrated ORR of 84% with 6% CR rate and median PFS of 22 months. In previously treated patients (n=105), ORR was 64% with median PFS of 16.5 months. For RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer, ORR was 73% in previously treated and 92% in treatment-naive patients. Selpercatinib showed robust intracranial activity with intracranial ORR of 91% in measurable CNS disease. The drug was well tolerated with dose reductions required in 30% of patients, primarily for hypertension, elevated liver enzymes, and dry mouth. LIBRETTO-001 established the paradigm of selective RET inhibition as superior to multikinase inhibitors and led to FDA approval of selpercatinib across multiple RET-driven indications, establishing RET as a key actionable oncogenic driver.

title: Sacituzumab Govitecan in Metastatic Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (ASCENT)
text_chunk: ASCENT was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing sacituzumab govitecan with physician's choice single-agent chemotherapy in patients with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer who had received at least two prior systemic therapies. The trial enrolled 529 patients. In the brain metastases-negative population, sacituzumab govitecan demonstrated dramatically improved PFS (5.6 vs 1.7 months, HR 0.41) and OS (12.1 vs 6.7 months, HR 0.48). ORR was 35% versus 5%. Sacituzumab govitecan targets TROP-2, expressed in over 90% of TNBC, via a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (SN-38) linked by a hydrolyzable CL2A linker enabling bystander killing. Key toxicities included neutropenia (grade 3+ in 51%), diarrhea (10% grade 3+), and febrile neutropenia (6%). UGT1A1*28 homozygosity was associated with increased neutropenia risk. ASCENT established sacituzumab govitecan as the new standard for pretreated metastatic TNBC.

title: Enfortumab Vedotin plus Pembrolizumab in Previously Untreated Advanced Urothelial Carcinoma (EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39)
text_chunk: EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab versus gemcitabine plus platinum-based chemotherapy as first-line treatment in 886 patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma. The combination demonstrated dramatically improved PFS (12.5 vs 6.3 months, HR 0.45, p<0.001) and OS (not reached vs 16.1 months, HR 0.47, p<0.001). ORR was 68% versus 44%, with CR rate of 29% versus 13%. Benefit was consistent across PD-L1 subgroups and cisplatin eligibility status. Key adverse effects included skin reactions, peripheral neuropathy, and hyperglycemia associated with enfortumab vedotin, and immune-related adverse events from pembrolizumab. EV-302 fundamentally changed first-line treatment of advanced urothelial carcinoma by establishing the ADC-immunotherapy paradigm as the new standard of care, displacing platinum-based chemotherapy.

title: Mirvetuximab Soravtansine in FRalpha-Positive Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer (MIRASOL)
text_chunk: MIRASOL (GOG-3045/ENGOT-ov55) was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing mirvetuximab soravtansine with investigator's choice chemotherapy in patients with FRalpha-positive (PS2+ by VENTANA FOLR1 26B3), platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian cancer. The trial enrolled 453 patients. Mirvetuximab soravtansine demonstrated improved PFS (5.6 vs 4.0 months, HR 0.65) and, importantly, a statistically significant OS improvement (16.5 vs 12.8 months, HR 0.67). ORR was 42.3% versus 15.9%. Mirvetuximab soravtansine is an ADC targeting folate receptor alpha, delivering the maytansinoid DM4 payload. The VENTANA FOLR1 (26B3) RxDx companion diagnostic identifies patients most likely to benefit. Ocular toxicity (blurred vision, keratopathy) was the most notable adverse event, occurring in approximately 40% of patients and requiring ophthalmologic monitoring. MIRASOL confirmed the accelerated approval from SORAYA and established mirvetuximab soravtansine as a new standard for FRalpha-positive platinum-resistant ovarian cancer.

title: Zolbetuximab plus mFOLFOX6 in CLDN18.2-Positive HER2-Negative Gastric or Gastroesophageal Junction Adenocarcinoma (SPOTLIGHT)
text_chunk: SPOTLIGHT was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating zolbetuximab plus mFOLFOX6 versus placebo plus mFOLFOX6 in 565 patients with CLDN18.2-positive (>=75% moderate-to-strong membranous staining), HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic gastric or GEJ adenocarcinoma. Zolbetuximab significantly improved both PFS (10.6 vs 8.7 months, HR 0.75, p=0.0066) and OS (18.2 vs 15.5 months, HR 0.75, p=0.0053). CLDN18.2 is a tight junction protein with restricted normal expression but frequently overexpressed in gastric cancer (approximately 38% of tumors meet the IHC threshold). The VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) assay is the companion diagnostic. Key adverse effects were GI-predominant: nausea (76%), vomiting (67%), and decreased appetite, most prominent in early cycles. SPOTLIGHT, together with the complementary GLOW trial, established CLDN18.2 as the first tight junction protein target in oncology and zolbetuximab as a new first-line option for eligible patients.

title: Tarlatamab for Patients with Previously Treated Small-Cell Lung Cancer (DeLLphi-301)
text_chunk: DeLLphi-301 was a phase 2, open-label, multicenter trial evaluating tarlatamab, a first-in-class DLL3-targeting bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE), in 220 patients with previously treated extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer. At the recommended dose of 10mg IV every 2 weeks, tarlatamab demonstrated ORR of 40%, median DOR of 9.7 months, and median OS of 14.3 months. These results are remarkable given the historically dismal outcomes for relapsed SCLC (median OS approximately 5-7 months with topotecan). DLL3 is a Notch ligand expressed on the surface of approximately 85% of SCLC tumors but minimally in normal tissues. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 53% (grade 3 in 1%), predominantly during cycle 1. ICANS occurred in 10%. Step-up dosing with inpatient monitoring for initial doses mitigated serious CRS risk. DeLLphi-301 led to FDA accelerated approval of tarlatamab and represented a paradigm shift in SCLC treatment through T-cell redirection.

title: Maintenance Olaparib in Patients with Newly Diagnosed Advanced Ovarian Cancer (SOLO-1)
text_chunk: SOLO-1 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of olaparib maintenance therapy in 391 patients with newly diagnosed, advanced BRCA1/2-mutant high-grade serous or endometrioid ovarian cancer who had a complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Olaparib dramatically improved median PFS to 56.0 months versus 13.8 months for placebo (HR 0.33, p<0.001). The 5-year PFS rate was 48% for olaparib versus 21% for placebo. The 7-year follow-up demonstrated sustained disease-free survival benefit with 67% of olaparib patients remaining disease-free at 2 years post-treatment completion versus 32% for placebo. Grade 3+ adverse events with olaparib included anemia (22%), fatigue (4%), and neutropenia (9%). SOLO-1 established PARP inhibitor maintenance as the standard of care for BRCA-mutant advanced ovarian cancer and demonstrated that early intervention can fundamentally alter the disease trajectory, with a substantial proportion of patients achieving prolonged remission.

title: Alectinib versus Crizotinib in Untreated ALK-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (ALEX)
text_chunk: ALEX was a pivotal phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing alectinib with crizotinib as first-line therapy for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. The trial enrolled 303 patients. Alectinib demonstrated superior investigator-assessed median PFS of 34.8 months versus 10.9 months for crizotinib (HR 0.43, p<0.001). Alectinib showed exceptional CNS activity with intracranial ORR of 81% and CNS PFS rate of 95% at 12 months (vs 60% for crizotinib). The 5-year OS rates were 62.5% for alectinib versus 45.5% for crizotinib (HR 0.67). Alectinib was better tolerated than crizotinib with lower rates of GI toxicity (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea). Grade 3-5 adverse events occurred in 41% with alectinib versus 50% with crizotinib. ALEX established alectinib as the NCCN-preferred first-line ALK inhibitor and set a new benchmark for molecularly targeted therapy, demonstrating that selecting the optimal first-line TKI substantially impacts long-term outcomes in ALK-positive NSCLC.

title: Combined BRAF and MEK Inhibition versus BRAF Inhibition Alone in Melanoma (COMBI-d)
text_chunk: COMBI-d was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing dabrafenib plus trametinib versus dabrafenib plus placebo in 423 patients with BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma. The combination demonstrated superior PFS (11.0 vs 8.8 months), ORR (69% vs 53%), and OS (25.1 vs 18.7 months, HR 0.71). The landmark 5-year OS rate was 34% for the combination, unprecedented for metastatic melanoma at that time. Vertical pathway blockade (BRAF+MEK) reduced paradoxical MAPK pathway activation, lowering the incidence of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (2% vs 9%). Pyrexia was the most distinctive adverse event of dabrafenib-containing regimens, occurring in approximately 50% of patients. COMBI-d, together with COMBI-v, established dual BRAF+MEK inhibition as the standard targeted therapy approach for BRAF V600-mutant melanoma and demonstrated that combination targeted therapy can achieve long-term survival in a meaningful proportion of patients.

title: Palbociclib and Fulvestrant in Advanced Breast Cancer (PALOMA-3)
text_chunk: PALOMA-3 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing palbociclib plus fulvestrant versus placebo plus fulvestrant in 521 patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer that had relapsed or progressed during prior endocrine therapy. Palbociclib plus fulvestrant significantly improved median PFS to 9.5 months versus 4.6 months for placebo plus fulvestrant (HR 0.46, p<0.001). Updated OS analysis showed improvement of 6.9 months (34.9 vs 28.0 months, HR 0.81, p=0.0221). Neutropenia was the most common grade 3+ adverse event (65%) but was rarely complicated by febrile neutropenia (0.6%). PALOMA-3 established CDK4/6 inhibition plus endocrine therapy as a transformative paradigm in HR-positive advanced breast cancer and was pivotal in the approval of palbociclib. The trial demonstrated that targeting the cell cycle through CDK4/6 inhibition can overcome endocrine resistance and extend the duration of endocrine-sensitive disease control.

title: Atezolizumab for First-Line Treatment of Metastatic Nonsquamous NSCLC (IMpower150)
text_chunk: IMpower150 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating atezolizumab plus bevacizumab plus carboplatin/paclitaxel (ABCP) versus bevacizumab plus chemotherapy (BCP) in 1,202 patients with first-line nonsquamous advanced NSCLC. ABCP demonstrated improved OS (19.2 vs 14.7 months, HR 0.78, p=0.02) and PFS (8.3 vs 6.8 months, HR 0.62). Importantly, IMpower150 was the first immunotherapy trial to include patients with EGFR mutations and ALK rearrangements who had progressed on TKI therapy, and showed PFS benefit in this subgroup. Benefit was also observed in patients with liver metastases. The addition of bevacizumab is hypothesized to enhance anti-tumor immunity through reversal of VEGF-mediated immunosuppression, promotion of T-cell trafficking, and normalization of tumor vasculature. IMpower150 established a unique four-drug regimen as a viable first-line option and provided an important treatment strategy for EGFR/ALK-positive NSCLC after TKI failure.

title: Encorafenib, Binimetinib, and Cetuximab in BRAF V600E-Mutated Colorectal Cancer (BEACON CRC)
text_chunk: BEACON CRC was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating encorafenib plus cetuximab (doublet) and encorafenib plus binimetinib plus cetuximab (triplet) versus investigator's choice chemotherapy (FOLFIRI or irinotecan plus cetuximab) in 665 patients with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic CRC after one or two prior therapies. The triplet demonstrated OS of 9.3 months versus 5.9 months for control (HR 0.52, p<0.001) and ORR of 27% versus 2%. The doublet showed similar efficacy to the triplet with better tolerability, becoming the preferred clinical regimen. BEACON CRC established that BRAF V600E-mutant CRC, unlike melanoma, requires concurrent EGFR inhibition to prevent adaptive feedback reactivation of the MAPK pathway through RTK-mediated RAS activation. This trial transformed the treatment of BRAF V600E-mutant CRC, which carries the worst prognosis among CRC molecular subtypes with median OS of approximately 12 months from diagnosis of metastatic disease.

title: Alpelisib plus Fulvestrant in PIK3CA-Mutated HR-Positive Advanced Breast Cancer (SOLAR-1)
text_chunk: SOLAR-1 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing alpelisib plus fulvestrant versus placebo plus fulvestrant in 572 patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer previously treated with endocrine therapy. In the PIK3CA-mutant cohort (n=341), alpelisib plus fulvestrant improved PFS to 11.0 months versus 5.7 months (HR 0.65, p<0.001). ORR was 26.6% versus 12.8%. No benefit was seen in the PIK3CA-wild-type cohort, establishing PIK3CA mutation as a predictive biomarker. PIK3CA mutations are found in approximately 40% of HR-positive breast cancers. Key adverse events included hyperglycemia (64%, grade 3+ in 37%), diarrhea (58%), and rash (52%). Hyperglycemia management with metformin and insulin was essential. SOLAR-1 led to FDA approval of alpelisib with companion diagnostic testing for PIK3CA mutations and established PI3K pathway targeting as a precision medicine approach in endocrine-resistant HR-positive breast cancer.

title: Venetoclax and Azacitidine in Previously Untreated Acute Myeloid Leukemia (VIALE-A)
text_chunk: VIALE-A was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial comparing venetoclax plus azacitidine versus placebo plus azacitidine in 431 patients with newly diagnosed AML who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy due to age (>=75 years) or comorbidities. The combination significantly improved both OS (14.7 vs 9.6 months, HR 0.66, p<0.001) and CR+CRi rate (66.4% vs 28.3%, p<0.001). Median time to first response was 1.3 months. The benefit was observed across molecular subgroups including IDH1/2-mutant, FLT3-mutant, and NPM1-mutant AML, with the most pronounced benefit in IDH1/2-mutant patients. Grade 3+ hematologic adverse events were common (neutropenia 42%, thrombocytopenia 45%, febrile neutropenia 42%), requiring cycle delays and dose modifications. VIALE-A established venetoclax plus azacitidine as the new standard of care for older or unfit patients with AML, transforming a historically difficult-to-treat population with previously limited options.

title: Capivasertib plus Fulvestrant in Breast Cancer with AKT1, PIK3CA, or PTEN Alterations (CAPItello-291)
text_chunk: CAPItello-291 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing capivasertib plus fulvestrant versus placebo plus fulvestrant in 708 patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer after progression on aromatase inhibitor with or without CDK4/6 inhibitor. In the PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN-altered population (~40%), PFS was 7.3 versus 3.1 months (HR 0.50, p<0.001). In the overall population, PFS was 5.5 versus 3.6 months (HR 0.60). Capivasertib is an oral, selective AKT inhibitor administered on an intermittent 4-days-on/3-days-off schedule to manage toxicity. Key adverse events included diarrhea (72%), rash (47%), and hyperglycemia (16%). The FoundationOne CDx companion diagnostic identifies patients with PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN alterations. CAPItello-291 established AKT inhibition as a viable precision medicine strategy in endocrine-resistant breast cancer and provided a new option for patients who had progressed on CDK4/6 inhibitors.

title: Elacestrant versus Standard of Care for Estrogen Receptor-Positive, HER2-Negative Advanced Breast Cancer (EMERALD)
text_chunk: EMERALD was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing elacestrant with investigator's choice standard-of-care endocrine monotherapy in 478 patients with ER-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer who had received one or two prior lines of endocrine therapy including a CDK4/6 inhibitor. In the ESR1-mutant population (~48%), elacestrant demonstrated PFS of 3.8 versus 1.9 months (HR 0.55, p=0.0005). In the overall population, PFS was 2.8 versus 1.9 months (HR 0.70, p=0.002). ESR1 mutations are acquired mechanisms of resistance to aromatase inhibitors, found in 30-40% of metastatic ER-positive breast cancers. Elacestrant is an oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that retains activity against ESR1-mutant receptors. Key adverse events included nausea (35%), fatigue (19%), and vomiting (19%). EMERALD led to FDA approval of elacestrant for ESR1-mutant breast cancer and established ESR1 mutation (detected by Guardant360 CDx ctDNA assay) as a predictive biomarker for oral SERD benefit.

title: Repotrectinib in ROS1 Fusion-Positive Advanced Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (TRIDENT-1)
text_chunk: TRIDENT-1 was a phase 1/2, multicenter, open-label trial of repotrectinib in patients with ROS1-positive or NTRK-positive advanced solid tumors. In 71 ROS1 TKI-naive NSCLC patients, repotrectinib achieved ORR of 79% with median DOR not reached at 15.7 months follow-up. In 56 patients previously treated with one ROS1 TKI, ORR was 38%, and specifically against the G2032R solvent-front resistance mutation, ORR was 59%. Repotrectinib's compact macrocyclic structure enables it to fit within the constrained binding pocket created by G2032R and other resistance mutations, providing a structural advantage over earlier-generation inhibitors. Key adverse effects included dizziness (58%), dysgeusia (50%), and constipation (35%), generally grade 1-2 and transient. TRIDENT-1 led to FDA approval of repotrectinib for ROS1-positive NSCLC in both TKI-naive and pretreated settings, establishing it as a next-generation option with unique resistance-mutation coverage.

title: Dostarlimab for Deficient Mismatch Repair Recurrent or Advanced Endometrial Cancer (GARNET)
text_chunk: The GARNET trial was a phase 1, open-label, multicohort study evaluating dostarlimab, an anti-PD-1 antibody, in patients with advanced or recurrent solid tumors including a pivotal cohort of dMMR/MSI-H endometrial cancer. In 104 evaluable dMMR/MSI-H endometrial cancer patients who had progressed on or after a platinum-containing regimen, dostarlimab demonstrated ORR of 42.3% with CR rate of 12.5%. Median DOR was not reached at median follow-up of 27.6 months, indicating highly durable responses. The disease control rate was 56.7%. Grade 3+ treatment-related adverse events occurred in 12.5% of patients, predominantly immune-mediated (thyroid dysfunction, colitis, hepatitis). GARNET led to FDA accelerated approval of dostarlimab for dMMR recurrent or advanced endometrial cancer after progression on prior platinum chemotherapy, and subsequently for dMMR recurrent or advanced solid tumors. The trial reinforced universal MMR/MSI testing in endometrial cancer.

title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Previously Treated HER2-Low Advanced Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast04)
text_chunk: DESTINY-Breast04 was a landmark phase 3, randomized trial comparing trastuzumab deruxtecan with physician's choice chemotherapy in 557 patients with HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-negative) metastatic breast cancer after one or two prior chemotherapy lines. In the HR-positive cohort (n=494), T-DXd improved PFS to 10.1 versus 5.4 months (HR 0.51, p<0.001) and OS to 23.9 versus 17.5 months (HR 0.64, p=0.003). In the overall population (including HR-negative/HER2-low), PFS was 9.9 versus 5.1 months and OS was 23.4 versus 16.8 months. This trial fundamentally redefined HER2 as an actionable target beyond the traditional HER2-positive definition, creating a new HER2-low category that encompasses approximately 55% of all breast cancers. The bystander killing effect of the DXd payload enables activity even in tumors with heterogeneous low-level HER2 expression. ILD occurred in 12.1% (grade 5 in 0.8%), requiring vigilant monitoring. DESTINY-Breast04 led to FDA approval of T-DXd for HER2-low breast cancer.

title: Osimertinib in Resected EGFR-Mutated Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (ADAURA)
text_chunk: ADAURA was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating adjuvant osimertinib versus placebo in 682 patients with completely resected stage IB-IIIA EGFR-mutant (exon 19 deletion or L858R) NSCLC after adjuvant chemotherapy (if given). Osimertinib demonstrated a dramatic improvement in disease-free survival: in stage II-IIIA patients, 3-year DFS was 84% versus 34% for placebo (HR 0.17, p<0.001). In the overall population (IB-IIIA), 3-year DFS was 80% versus 28% (HR 0.20). Crucially, the OS analysis published in 2023 showed a significant improvement with 5-year OS of 88% versus 78% for stage II-IIIA (HR 0.49, p<0.001). CNS disease recurrence was dramatically reduced (2% vs 10%). ADAURA established osimertinib as the first and only adjuvant targeted therapy to demonstrate OS benefit in resected NSCLC, fundamentally changing the management of early-stage EGFR-mutant lung cancer and establishing the principle of biomarker-driven adjuvant therapy in thoracic oncology.

title: Capmatinib in MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutated Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (GEOMETRY mono-1)
text_chunk: GEOMETRY mono-1 was a multicenter, nonrandomized, open-label, multicohort phase 2 study of capmatinib in patients with advanced NSCLC harboring MET alterations. In 28 treatment-naive patients with MET exon 14 skipping mutations, capmatinib demonstrated ORR of 68% (CR 4%), median DOR of 12.6 months, and median PFS of 12.4 months. In 69 previously treated patients, ORR was 41% with median PFS of 5.4 months. In MET-amplified tumors (GCN>=10), ORR was 29% in the second-line+ setting. Capmatinib showed intracranial activity with intracranial ORR of 54% in patients with brain metastases. MET exon 14 skipping mutations are found in approximately 3-4% of NSCLC, enriched in elderly patients and sarcomatoid histology. Key adverse events included peripheral edema (51%), nausea (45%), and vomiting (28%). GEOMETRY mono-1 established MET exon 14 skipping as a distinct actionable driver in NSCLC and led to FDA approval of capmatinib, providing a new targeted therapy option for this molecular subset.

title: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab in Microsatellite-Instability-High Metastatic Colorectal Cancer (CheckMate-8HW)
text_chunk: CheckMate-8HW was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus nivolumab monotherapy versus investigator's choice chemotherapy in patients with first-line MSI-H/dMMR metastatic colorectal cancer. In the dual immunotherapy versus chemotherapy comparison, nivolumab plus ipilimumab demonstrated significantly superior PFS (not reached vs 5.9 months, HR 0.21, p<0.001) with a 24-month PFS rate of 72% for the combination. ORR was 69% versus 38% with a CR rate of 34% versus 7%. In the head-to-head immunotherapy comparison, nivolumab plus ipilimumab was also superior to nivolumab monotherapy (PFS HR 0.62). The combination showed consistent benefit across subgroups including BRAF V600E-mutant, RAS-mutant, and prior adjuvant therapy. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 23% with the combination. CheckMate-8HW established dual checkpoint blockade as potentially the most effective first-line approach for MSI-H mCRC, raising the bar beyond the pembrolizumab monotherapy established by KEYNOTE-177.

title: Midostaurin Added to Chemotherapy in FLT3-Mutant Acute Myeloid Leukemia (RATIFY/CALGB 10603)
text_chunk: The RATIFY trial (CALGB 10603) was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study evaluating midostaurin added to standard induction (daunorubicin plus cytarabine) and consolidation chemotherapy in 717 patients aged 18-59 with newly diagnosed FLT3-mutant AML (both ITD and TKD mutations). Midostaurin significantly improved overall survival with a median OS of 74.7 months versus 25.6 months for placebo (HR 0.78, one-sided p=0.009). The 4-year OS rate was 51.4% versus 44.3%. CR rates were similar between arms (58.9% vs 53.5%), but midostaurin improved event-free survival (HR 0.78). The benefit was observed in both FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD subgroups, though the FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio group derived the greatest benefit. Midostaurin is a multikinase inhibitor that also targets KIT, PDGFR, and VEGFR. Grade 3+ rash (14%) and nausea were notable adverse events. RATIFY established FLT3 mutation testing as mandatory at AML diagnosis and midostaurin plus chemotherapy as the standard first-line treatment for FLT3-mutant AML in younger fit patients, leading to FDA approval in 2017.

title: Imatinib Compared with Interferon and Low-Dose Cytarabine for Newly Diagnosed Chronic-Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (IRIS)
text_chunk: The IRIS trial was a landmark phase 3, randomized, open-label study comparing imatinib (400mg daily) with interferon-alpha plus low-dose cytarabine in 1,106 patients with newly diagnosed chronic-phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). At 18 months, imatinib demonstrated a complete cytogenetic response (CCyR) rate of 76.2% versus 14.5% for interferon-based therapy (p<0.001). The estimated 5-year OS rate was 89% for imatinib. By 10 years of follow-up, the CCyR rate reached 83%, and the estimated OS was 83.3%. Major molecular response (MMR, BCR-ABL1/ABL1 <=0.1%) was achieved in 86% of patients by 5 years. Imatinib was remarkably well tolerated compared to interferon, with primary adverse events including superficial edema (56%), nausea (44%), muscle cramps (38%), and rash (33%). Grade 3-4 neutropenia occurred in 14%. The IRIS trial transformed CML from a fatal disease to a manageable chronic condition, established the paradigm of targeted therapy for oncogene-addicted cancers, and validated BCR-ABL1 as a therapeutic target. Imatinib remains a cornerstone of CML therapy alongside second-generation TKIs.

title: Ruxolitinib versus Best Available Therapy in Myelofibrosis (COMFORT-I and COMFORT-II)
text_chunk: The COMFORT studies were two parallel phase 3 trials evaluating ruxolitinib, a JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor, in patients with intermediate-2 or high-risk myelofibrosis. COMFORT-I (n=309) compared ruxolitinib with placebo, and COMFORT-II (n=219) compared ruxolitinib with best available therapy (BAT) in patients with primary myelofibrosis, post-polycythemia vera myelofibrosis, or post-essential thrombocythemia myelofibrosis. In COMFORT-I, 41.9% of ruxolitinib patients achieved >=35% spleen volume reduction at 24 weeks versus 0.7% for placebo (p<0.001). Ruxolitinib also significantly improved total symptom score and quality of life. Long-term follow-up of COMFORT-I showed an OS advantage for ruxolitinib (median OS 5.3 vs 3.8 years, HR 0.69). The benefit was observed regardless of JAK2 V617F mutation status, though JAK2-mutant patients had numerically greater spleen responses. Key adverse events included anemia (31% grade 3+) and thrombocytopenia (13% grade 3+), reflecting on-target JAK-mediated myelosuppression. The COMFORT trials established ruxolitinib as the first approved therapy for myelofibrosis and validated JAK-STAT pathway targeting in myeloproliferative neoplasms.

title: Ibrutinib versus Ofatumumab in Previously Treated Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (RESONATE)
text_chunk: RESONATE was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing ibrutinib, a first-in-class covalent BTK inhibitor, with ofatumumab (anti-CD20 antibody) in 391 patients with relapsed or refractory chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) or small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL) who had received at least one prior therapy. Ibrutinib dramatically improved PFS (median not reached vs 8.1 months, HR 0.22, p<0.001) and OS (1-year OS 90% vs 81%, HR 0.43, p=0.005). ORR was 63% versus 4%. Notably, ibrutinib benefit was observed across high-risk subgroups including del(17p)/TP53 mutation, unmutated IGHV, and del(11q). At 6-year follow-up, median PFS was 44.1 months. Key adverse events included atrial fibrillation (11%), bleeding events (44%, mostly grade 1-2), diarrhea (48%), and arthralgia (27%). RESONATE established ibrutinib as a transformative therapy for relapsed CLL, demonstrating that continuous oral BTK inhibition could replace chemoimmunotherapy and offering benefit to the highest-risk genetic subgroups with the worst outcomes on conventional therapy.

title: Zanubrutinib versus Ibrutinib in Relapsed/Refractory CLL (ALPINE)
text_chunk: ALPINE was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing zanubrutinib, a next-generation selective BTK inhibitor, with ibrutinib in 652 patients with relapsed or refractory CLL/SLL who had received at least one prior therapy. Zanubrutinib demonstrated superior ORR (80.4% vs 72.9%, p=0.006) and a trend toward improved PFS (HR 0.65 at 24-month analysis). Importantly, zanubrutinib showed a significantly lower rate of atrial fibrillation/flutter (2.5% vs 10.1%, p<0.001) and lower rates of major hemorrhage (3.4% vs 5.5%) and hypertension (14.8% vs 14.1%). The improved cardiac safety profile of zanubrutinib is attributed to its greater selectivity for BTK over off-target kinases including TEC family kinases and EGFR. Zanubrutinib showed consistent benefit in high-risk subgroups including del(17p) and unmutated IGHV. ALPINE established zanubrutinib as a BTK inhibitor with an improved safety profile compared to ibrutinib, leading to FDA approval of zanubrutinib for CLL/SLL, and supporting the concept that next-generation selective inhibitors can maintain or improve efficacy while reducing off-target toxicity.

title: Olaparib for Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer with Homologous Recombination Repair Gene Alterations (PROfound)
text_chunk: PROfound was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing olaparib with physician's choice of enzalutamide or abiraterone in 387 patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) harboring alterations in homologous recombination repair (HRR) genes who had progressed on a prior novel hormonal agent. Cohort A included BRCA1, BRCA2, or ATM alterations; Cohort B included alterations in 12 other HRR genes. In Cohort A (n=245), olaparib significantly improved median PFS to 7.4 versus 3.6 months (HR 0.34, p<0.001) and median OS to 19.1 versus 14.7 months (HR 0.69, p=0.02). The benefit was most pronounced in BRCA2-altered patients (PFS HR 0.21). In the overall population (Cohorts A+B), PFS was 5.8 versus 3.5 months (HR 0.49). Key adverse events included anemia (46%, grade 3+ in 21%), nausea (41%), and fatigue (41%). PROfound established olaparib as the first PARP inhibitor approved for mCRPC and mandated HRR gene testing in advanced prostate cancer, bringing precision medicine to one of the most common male cancers.

title: 177Lu-PSMA-617 in Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer (VISION)
text_chunk: VISION was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating 177Lu-PSMA-617 plus standard of care versus standard of care alone in 831 patients with PSMA-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer who had progressed after at least one androgen receptor pathway inhibitor and one or two taxane-based chemotherapy regimens. PSMA positivity was determined by 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT (PSMA-positive defined as uptake greater than liver in at least one lesion with no PSMA-negative bone lesions >2cm). 177Lu-PSMA-617 significantly improved both PFS (median 8.7 vs 3.4 months, HR 0.40, p<0.001) and OS (median 15.3 vs 11.3 months, HR 0.62, p<0.001). PSA response rate (>=50% decline) was 46% versus 7%. Key adverse events included fatigue (43%), dry mouth (39%), nausea (35%), and hematologic toxicity (anemia 32%, thrombocytopenia 17%). VISION established radioligand therapy as a new modality in prostate cancer and led to FDA approval of 177Lu-PSMA-617, the first PSMA-directed radioligand therapy, creating a new treatment paradigm for late-line mCRPC.

title: Enzalutamide in Nonmetastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer (PROSPER)
text_chunk: PROSPER was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating enzalutamide plus androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) versus placebo plus ADT in 1,401 patients with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (nmCRPC) with a rapidly rising PSA (PSA doubling time <=10 months). Enzalutamide significantly improved metastasis-free survival (MFS) to 36.6 months versus 14.7 months for placebo (HR 0.29, p<0.001). Updated OS analysis demonstrated significant OS improvement (67.0 vs 56.3 months, HR 0.73, p=0.001). Time to PSA progression was 37.2 versus 3.9 months. Key adverse events included fatigue (33%), hypertension (12%), falls (11%), and seizures (<1%). Enzalutamide is a second-generation androgen receptor inhibitor that blocks AR nuclear translocation, DNA binding, and co-activator recruitment. PROSPER, together with the SPARTAN trial (apalutamide), established next-generation AR inhibitors as standard of care in nmCRPC, a disease state previously managed with watchful waiting, and demonstrated that delaying metastatic progression translates into meaningful OS benefit.

title: 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus Cabazitaxel in mCRPC (TheraP)
text_chunk: TheraP was a phase 2, randomized, open-label trial comparing 177Lu-PSMA-617 with cabazitaxel in 200 patients with PSMA-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer who had progressed after docetaxel. PSMA positivity required SUVmax >=20 on 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET with no FDG-positive/PSMA-negative discordant sites. 177Lu-PSMA-617 demonstrated a higher PSA50 response rate (66% vs 37%, p<0.001) and superior PFS (HR 0.69). The ORR by RECIST was 49% versus 24%. Grade 3-4 adverse events were less frequent with 177Lu-PSMA-617 (33% vs 53%), with dry mouth, nausea, and fatigue being the main toxicities versus neutropenia and diarrhea with cabazitaxel. TheraP was notable for using dual PSMA and FDG PET imaging for patient selection, demonstrating that PSMA-negative/FDG-positive discordant disease (present in approximately 15% of screened patients) predicts poor response to PSMA radioligand therapy. TheraP provided randomized evidence supporting 177Lu-PSMA-617 as superior to standard chemotherapy in the post-docetaxel mCRPC setting with better efficacy and tolerability.

title: Atezolizumab plus Bevacizumab in Unresectable Hepatocellular Carcinoma (IMbrave150)
text_chunk: IMbrave150 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing atezolizumab plus bevacizumab with sorafenib in 501 patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) who had not received prior systemic therapy. The combination demonstrated significantly improved OS (median 19.2 vs 13.4 months, HR 0.66, p<0.001) and PFS (6.9 vs 4.3 months, HR 0.65). ORR by RECIST 1.1 was 30% versus 11%, with a CR rate of 8% versus <1%. Importantly, atezolizumab plus bevacizumab improved patient-reported quality of life and delayed deterioration of functioning. The combination was effective across etiologies (HBV, HCV, non-viral) and PD-L1 subgroups. The addition of bevacizumab addresses VEGF-mediated immunosuppression in the hepatic microenvironment and reduces portal hypertension. Key adverse events included hypertension (30%), proteinuria (20%), and upper GI bleeding (7%, requiring adequate variceal management). IMbrave150 ended the decade-long sorafenib monotherapy era in first-line HCC and established I/O-based combination as the new standard of care.

title: Durvalumab plus Tremelimumab for Unresectable Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HIMALAYA)
text_chunk: HIMALAYA was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating the STRIDE regimen (single tremelimumab regular interval durvalumab: one dose of tremelimumab 300mg plus durvalumab, then durvalumab monotherapy Q4W), durvalumab monotherapy, and sorafenib in 1,171 patients with unresectable HCC without prior systemic therapy. The STRIDE regimen demonstrated superior OS versus sorafenib (median 16.4 vs 13.8 months, HR 0.78, p=0.0035). The 3-year OS rate was 30.7% for STRIDE versus 20.2% for sorafenib. ORR was 20.1% versus 5.1%, with durable responses (median DOR not reached for STRIDE). Durvalumab monotherapy was noninferior to sorafenib for OS (16.6 vs 13.8 months, HR 0.86), providing an additional option. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 25.8% for STRIDE versus 36.9% for sorafenib. The single priming dose of anti-CTLA-4 was designed to enhance antitumor T-cell response while limiting immune-related toxicity. HIMALAYA established the STRIDE regimen as an alternative first-line option for advanced HCC, particularly for patients not eligible for bevacizumab (e.g., those with high variceal bleeding risk).

title: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab for Intermediate and Poor-Risk Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma (CheckMate-214)
text_chunk: CheckMate-214 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing nivolumab plus ipilimumab with sunitinib in 1,096 patients with previously untreated advanced or metastatic renal cell carcinoma (RCC). In intermediate- and poor-risk patients (per IMDC criteria, n=847), the combination demonstrated superior OS (median not reached vs 26.0 months at initial analysis, HR 0.63, p<0.001) and ORR (42% vs 27%, with CR rate of 9% vs 1%). At 5-year follow-up, median OS was 47.0 versus 26.6 months (HR 0.68). Notably, complete responses were durable with 10 of 52 CR patients remaining in CR off all therapy at 5 years. PD-L1>=1% patients derived the greatest benefit (ORR 58% vs 25%). In favorable-risk patients, sunitinib remained superior for PFS. Key adverse events included fatigue (46%), pruritis (28%), diarrhea (27%), and immune-mediated hepatitis, colitis, and endocrinopathies. CheckMate-214 established dual checkpoint blockade as the first-line standard for intermediate/poor-risk mRCC and demonstrated the potential for long-term treatment-free remission with immunotherapy combinations.

title: Pembrolizumab plus Axitinib versus Sunitinib for First-Line Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma (KEYNOTE-426)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-426 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing pembrolizumab plus axitinib with sunitinib in 861 patients with previously untreated advanced clear cell RCC across all IMDC risk groups. The combination demonstrated improved OS (median not reached vs 35.7 months at initial analysis, HR 0.68, p<0.001), PFS (15.4 vs 11.1 months, HR 0.69, p<0.001), and ORR (59.3% vs 35.7%). Unlike CheckMate-214, KEYNOTE-426 showed benefit across all IMDC risk categories including favorable-risk patients. At 42-month follow-up, OS continued to favor the combination (HR 0.73). CR rate was 10% versus 4%. Key adverse events included diarrhea (56%), hypertension (44%), fatigue (38%), hepatotoxicity (from both pembrolizumab and axitinib), and immune-related events. KEYNOTE-426 established I/O plus TKI as a broadly applicable first-line option for mRCC, effective across risk categories and PD-L1 subgroups, and supported the concept that VEGF-TKI synergizes with PD-1 blockade through modulation of the tumor immune microenvironment.

title: Lenvatinib plus Pembrolizumab for Advanced Renal Cell Carcinoma (CLEAR/KEYNOTE-581)
text_chunk: CLEAR (KEYNOTE-581) was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab, lenvatinib plus everolimus, and sunitinib in 1,069 patients with previously untreated advanced clear cell RCC. Lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab demonstrated superior PFS (23.9 vs 9.2 months, HR 0.39, p<0.001), OS (HR 0.66, p=0.005), and ORR (71% vs 36.1%) compared with sunitinib. The CR rate was 16.1% versus 4.2%. The combination showed consistent benefit across all IMDC risk groups, PD-L1 subgroups, and in patients with sarcomatoid features. The PFS of 23.9 months and ORR of 71% represented the highest efficacy reported in first-line mRCC trials at the time. Key adverse events included diarrhea (62%), hypertension (56%), hypothyroidism (47%), and proteinuria (30%), requiring frequent dose modifications. Lenvatinib targets VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRA, KIT, and RET. CLEAR positioned lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab as a leading first-line option for mRCC, particularly for patients where high response rate and tumor shrinkage are treatment priorities.

title: Belzutifan for Von Hippel-Lindau Disease-Associated Renal Cell Carcinoma
text_chunk: Study 004 was a phase 2, open-label trial evaluating belzutifan, a first-in-class HIF-2alpha inhibitor, in 61 patients with VHL disease-associated clear cell RCC who were not candidates for surgery. VHL disease is caused by germline VHL loss-of-function mutations leading to constitutive activation of the HIF-alpha transcription factors that drive angiogenesis and tumor growth. Belzutifan demonstrated ORR of 49% in RCC tumors, with 56% reduction in sum of target lesion diameters. No patients progressed in RCC tumors. Belzutifan also showed activity in non-RCC VHL-associated tumors: CNS hemangioblastomas (30% ORR) and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (77% ORR). Key adverse events included anemia (90%, grade 3 in 7%), fatigue (47%), and hypoxia (1.6%). Anemia is an on-target pharmacodynamic effect of HIF-2alpha inhibition (erythropoietin reduction). Belzutifan received FDA approval for VHL disease-associated RCC and other tumors not requiring immediate surgery, representing a landmark first-in-class approval targeting the HIF pathway and demonstrating the power of targeting the root molecular cause of hereditary cancer syndromes.

title: Ripretinib in Advanced Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumor After Treatment with 3 or More Kinase Inhibitors (INVICTUS)
text_chunk: INVICTUS was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating ripretinib in 129 patients with advanced gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) who had received at least 3 prior kinase inhibitors (imatinib, sunitinib, and regorafenib). Ripretinib significantly improved median PFS to 6.3 versus 1.0 months for placebo (HR 0.15, p<0.001) and demonstrated a trend toward improved OS (15.1 vs 6.6 months, HR 0.36). ORR was 9.4% versus 0%. Ripretinib is a broad-spectrum switch-control kinase inhibitor designed to inhibit a wide range of KIT and PDGFRA mutations, including primary mutations (exon 9, 11, 13, 17) and secondary resistance mutations in the activation loop (exon 17/18) and ATP-binding pocket (exon 13/14). The drug was well tolerated with the most common adverse events being alopecia (52%), fatigue (42%), nausea (39%), and myalgia (32%). Palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia occurred in 21%. INVICTUS established ripretinib as the standard fourth-line therapy for advanced GIST and validated the concept of pan-KIT/PDGFRA inhibition to overcome heterogeneous secondary resistance mutations that emerge during sequential kinase inhibitor therapy.

title: Avapritinib in PDGFRA D842V-Mutant Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumor (NAVIGATOR)
text_chunk: NAVIGATOR was a phase 1 registration study evaluating avapritinib in patients with PDGFRA D842V-mutant gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). PDGFRA D842V is the most common PDGFRA mutation in GIST (approximately 5-6% of all GIST), and tumors harboring this mutation are intrinsically resistant to imatinib, sunitinib, and regorafenib. In 43 evaluable patients with PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST, avapritinib demonstrated an unprecedented ORR of 91% with a CR rate of 9%. Median DOR was not reached at 27 months of follow-up. The ctDNA clearance rate was 98%, indicating deep molecular responses. Avapritinib is a type I kinase inhibitor specifically designed to target PDGFRA D842V by binding the active conformation of the kinase. Key adverse events included periorbital edema (63%), nausea (51%), and cognitive effects (40%). Grade 3+ intracranial hemorrhage occurred in 1.4%. NAVIGATOR led to FDA approval of avapritinib for PDGFRA exon 18 mutant GIST (including D842V), transforming a previously untreatable molecular subtype into one with the highest response rates in GIST therapeutic history.

title: Pembrolizumab with Chemotherapy for Persistent, Recurrent, or Metastatic Cervical Cancer (KEYNOTE-826)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-826 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin plus paclitaxel, with or without bevacizumab) versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 617 patients with persistent, recurrent, or metastatic cervical cancer not previously treated with chemotherapy. Pembrolizumab significantly improved PFS (10.4 vs 8.2 months, HR 0.65, p<0.001) and OS (24-month OS 53% vs 41.7%, HR 0.64, p<0.001). ORR was 68.1% versus 50.2%. The benefit was observed regardless of PD-L1 CPS status, although patients with CPS>=1 and CPS>=10 derived the greatest benefit. Approximately 90% of cervical cancers are HPV-associated, contributing to higher immunogenicity. Key adverse events included anemia, neutropenia, and immune-related events (hypothyroidism 17%, colitis, hepatitis). KEYNOTE-826 established pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy as the new first-line standard for advanced cervical cancer, the first positive immunotherapy trial in this disease, and led to FDA approval of pembrolizumab in this setting.

title: Tisotumab Vedotin in Previously Treated Recurrent or Metastatic Cervical Cancer (innovaTV 204/GOG-3023)
text_chunk: innovaTV 204 (GOG-3023) was a phase 2, open-label, multicenter trial evaluating tisotumab vedotin, a tissue factor (TF)-directed antibody-drug conjugate with an MMAE payload, in 101 patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer who had progressed after one or two prior lines of systemic therapy including doublet chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab. Tisotumab vedotin demonstrated ORR of 24% (7% CR, 17% PR) with a median DOR of 8.3 months. Median OS was 12.1 months. Tissue factor is overexpressed in the majority of cervical cancers. Key adverse events included alopecia (38%), epistaxis (30%), conjunctivitis (26%), nausea (25%), and fatigue (24%). Ocular toxicity was notable, including conjunctival adverse events in 53% of patients, requiring cooling eye packs during infusion and lubricating eye drops. Peripheral neuropathy occurred in 26%. innovaTV 204 led to FDA accelerated approval of tisotumab vedotin for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer after prior chemotherapy, providing the first ADC approved in this disease setting.

title: Pembrolizumab for PD-L1-Positive Recurrent/Metastatic Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (KEYNOTE-048)
text_chunk: KEYNOTE-048 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating pembrolizumab monotherapy, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (5-FU plus cisplatin or carboplatin), and cetuximab plus chemotherapy (EXTREME regimen) in 882 patients with previously untreated recurrent or metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC). In the PD-L1 CPS>=20 population, pembrolizumab monotherapy demonstrated superior OS versus cetuximab-chemo (14.9 vs 10.7 months, HR 0.61, p=0.0007). In the CPS>=1 population, pembrolizumab plus chemo showed superior OS (13.0 vs 10.7 months, HR 0.65, p<0.001). Pembrolizumab monotherapy in CPS>=1 patients showed OS of 12.3 versus 10.3 months (HR 0.78, p=0.0086). The trial established a PD-L1-based treatment algorithm for first-line R/M HNSCC: pembrolizumab monotherapy for CPS>=20 (to spare chemotherapy toxicity) and pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy for CPS>=1 (where chemotherapy adds benefit). For CPS<1, cetuximab-based chemotherapy remained standard. KEYNOTE-048 fundamentally changed the first-line management of R/M HNSCC.

title: Nivolumab for Recurrent Squamous-Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck (CheckMate-141)
text_chunk: CheckMate-141 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing nivolumab with investigator's choice therapy (methotrexate, docetaxel, or cetuximab) in 361 patients with recurrent HNSCC who had progressed within 6 months of platinum-based chemotherapy. Nivolumab significantly improved OS to 7.5 versus 5.1 months (HR 0.70, p=0.01). The 1-year OS rate was 36% versus 17%. ORR was 13.3% versus 5.8%, but responders on nivolumab had remarkably durable responses (median DOR not reached vs 5.8 months). Benefit was observed regardless of HPV status, although HPV-positive patients had numerically better outcomes. PD-L1>=1% patients showed a trend toward greater benefit (HR 0.55). Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events were lower with nivolumab (13.1% vs 35.1%). CheckMate-141 established nivolumab as the first approved immunotherapy for second-line R/M HNSCC and demonstrated that checkpoint inhibitors could achieve durable responses in a disease with historically poor prognosis after platinum failure.

title: Dostarlimab plus Chemotherapy for Primary Advanced or Recurrent Endometrial Cancer (RUBY/ENGOT-EN6-NSGO)
text_chunk: RUBY (ENGOT-EN6-NSGO/GOG-3031) was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing dostarlimab plus carboplatin-paclitaxel followed by dostarlimab maintenance versus placebo plus chemotherapy followed by placebo in 494 patients with primary advanced (stage III-IV) or first recurrence of endometrial cancer. In the dMMR/MSI-H subpopulation (n=118), the addition of dostarlimab dramatically improved PFS (not reached vs 7.7 months, HR 0.28, p<0.001) and 24-month PFS rate (61.4% vs 15.7%). In the overall population, dostarlimab improved PFS (HR 0.64, p<0.001) with 24-month PFS of 36.1% versus 18.1%. OS trended in favor of dostarlimab (HR 0.64 in overall, HR 0.30 in dMMR). The dMMR subgroup showed the most profound benefit, consistent with the high immunogenicity of hypermutated dMMR tumors. Key adverse events included immune-related events (thyroid dysfunction, rash, colitis) added to chemotherapy toxicities. RUBY established front-line immunotherapy plus chemotherapy as the new standard for advanced endometrial cancer, particularly in the dMMR subset, and reinforced universal MMR testing in endometrial cancer.

title: Pembrolizumab plus Chemotherapy for Advanced Endometrial Cancer (NRG-GY018)
text_chunk: NRG-GY018 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial evaluating pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (carboplatin-paclitaxel) followed by pembrolizumab maintenance versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 816 patients with measurable stage III, IVA, or IVB or recurrent endometrial carcinoma, stratified by dMMR/MSI-H versus pMMR/MSS status. In the dMMR cohort (n=225), pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy dramatically improved PFS (not reached vs 7.6 months, HR 0.30, p<0.001). In the pMMR cohort (n=591), pembrolizumab also significantly improved PFS (13.1 vs 8.7 months, HR 0.54, p<0.001), demonstrating benefit beyond the dMMR population. This was a critical finding as approximately 75% of endometrial cancers are pMMR. The NRG-GY018 and RUBY trials together established front-line immunotherapy-chemotherapy as standard across endometrial cancer subtypes, with the most pronounced benefit in dMMR tumors. Key immune-related adverse events included thyroid disorders, rash, and colitis. NRG-GY018 led to FDA approval of pembrolizumab for endometrial carcinoma in combination with chemotherapy.

title: Gilteritinib versus Salvage Chemotherapy in Relapsed or Refractory FLT3-Mutated AML (ADMIRAL)
text_chunk: ADMIRAL was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing gilteritinib, a selective FLT3 inhibitor, with salvage chemotherapy (MEC, FLAG-IDA, LDAC, or azacitidine) in 371 patients with relapsed or refractory FLT3-mutant (ITD or TKD) AML. Gilteritinib significantly improved OS (median 9.3 vs 5.6 months, HR 0.64, p<0.001). The CR+CRh rate was 34.0% versus 15.3%. Median EFS was 2.8 versus 0.7 months. The benefit was observed in both FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD subgroups, and in patients who had relapsed after allogeneic stem cell transplant. Gilteritinib is a type I FLT3 inhibitor that targets both the active and inactive conformations, maintaining activity against resistance mutations that emerge during midostaurin or sorafenib therapy. Key adverse events included febrile neutropenia (46%), anemia (41%), and elevated liver enzymes (40%). Differentiation syndrome occurred in 3% of gilteritinib patients. ADMIRAL established gilteritinib as the standard of care for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutant AML, providing a targeted alternative to intensive salvage chemotherapy.

title: Ivosidenib plus Azacitidine versus Placebo plus Azacitidine in Newly Diagnosed IDH1-Mutant AML (AGILE)
text_chunk: AGILE was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing ivosidenib plus azacitidine versus placebo plus azacitidine in 146 patients with newly diagnosed IDH1-mutant AML who were ineligible for intensive induction chemotherapy. Ivosidenib plus azacitidine demonstrated a significant EFS benefit (HR 0.33, p=0.002), improved CR rate (47.2% vs 14.9%, p<0.001), and OS improvement (median 24.0 vs 7.9 months, HR 0.44, p=0.001). The CR+CRi rate was 53% versus 18%. Molecular responses were notable: 36% of patients in the ivosidenib arm achieved IDH1 mutation clearance. Key adverse events included differentiation syndrome (14% with ivosidenib, grade 3+ in 5%), QTc prolongation (10%), and nausea (42%). IDH1 mutations are found in approximately 6-10% of AML and produce the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate (D-2-HG), which impairs hematopoietic differentiation. AGILE established ivosidenib plus azacitidine as the preferred first-line therapy for IDH1-mutant AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, demonstrating the power of mutation-specific targeted therapy in this disease.

title: Olaparib Maintenance Therapy in Patients with Germline BRCA-Mutated Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer (POLO)
text_chunk: POLO was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing olaparib maintenance with placebo in 154 patients with germline BRCA1/2-mutant metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma whose disease had not progressed during first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Olaparib significantly improved median PFS to 7.4 versus 3.8 months (HR 0.53, p=0.004). The 2-year PFS rate was 22.1% versus 9.6%. ORR was 23.1% versus 11.5%. However, the final OS analysis did not show a significant difference (median 19.0 vs 19.2 months, HR 0.83, p=0.3487), potentially confounded by post-progression crossover and subsequent therapies. Key adverse events were fatigue (60%), nausea (45%), and anemia (27%, grade 3+ in 11%). Germline BRCA1/2 mutations are found in approximately 5-7% of pancreatic cancers, concentrated in the BRCA2 gene. POLO established olaparib as the first targeted and first maintenance therapy for pancreatic cancer, providing a meaningful PFS benefit with improved quality of life compared to chemotherapy continuation, though the OS results tempered enthusiasm and highlighted the aggressive biology of pancreatic cancer.

title: Atezolizumab and Nab-Paclitaxel in PD-L1-Positive Advanced Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (IMpassion131 - Negative Trial)
text_chunk: IMpassion131 was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing atezolizumab plus paclitaxel versus placebo plus paclitaxel in 651 patients with previously untreated, inoperable, locally advanced or metastatic triple-negative breast cancer. Unlike the positive IMpassion130 trial (atezolizumab plus nab-paclitaxel), IMpassion131 failed to meet its primary endpoint: PFS was not improved in the PD-L1-positive (IC >=1%) population (6.0 vs 5.7 months, HR 0.82, p=0.20) or the intention-to-treat population (5.7 vs 5.6 months, HR 0.86). OS showed a numerical disadvantage for atezolizumab in the ITT population (HR 1.12). The discrepancy between IMpassion130 and IMpassion131 remains debated but may relate to immunosuppressive effects of dexamethasone premedication with solvent-based paclitaxel, differences in taxane scheduling, or the nab-paclitaxel carrier enhancing tumor immune microenvironment remodeling. IMpassion131 is a critical negative trial that demonstrated the importance of chemotherapy backbone selection in chemoimmunotherapy combinations and led to withdrawal of the atezolizumab indication for TNBC.

title: Avelumab First-Line Maintenance for Advanced Urothelial Carcinoma (JAVELIN Bladder 100)
text_chunk: JAVELIN Bladder 100 was a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating avelumab maintenance plus best supportive care (BSC) versus BSC alone in 700 patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma who had not progressed after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy (4-6 cycles of gemcitabine plus cisplatin or carboplatin). Avelumab maintenance significantly improved OS in both the overall population (21.4 vs 14.3 months, HR 0.69, p=0.001) and the PD-L1-positive population (not reached vs 17.1 months, HR 0.56). PFS was also improved (3.7 vs 2.0 months, HR 0.62). The switch maintenance strategy addressed the unmet need in urothelial carcinoma where responses to first-line chemotherapy are not durable. Key adverse events included immune-related events (thyroid dysfunction 12%, rash 6%, colitis 2%). JAVELIN Bladder 100 established avelumab maintenance as the first practice-changing maintenance strategy in urothelial carcinoma and demonstrated that consolidation immunotherapy after chemotherapy can significantly extend survival, a concept now being tested in other cancer types.

title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-Mutant Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer (DESTINY-Lung02)
text_chunk: DESTINY-Lung02 was a phase 2, randomized, multicenter trial evaluating trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) at two dose levels (5.4 mg/kg and 6.4 mg/kg) in patients with HER2-mutant (ERBB2-mutant) previously treated metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer. In the 5.4 mg/kg cohort (n=102), T-DXd demonstrated ORR of 49.0% with median DOR of 16.8 months and median PFS of 9.9 months. In the 6.4 mg/kg cohort (n=50), ORR was 56.0% with median PFS of 15.4 months. The most common HER2 mutations were exon 20 insertions (particularly YVMA insertion), and T-DXd showed activity across HER2 mutation subtypes. Key adverse events included nausea (63%), neutropenia (43%), and ILD (5.9% at 5.4 mg/kg, 14.0% at 6.4 mg/kg), leading to selection of the 5.4 mg/kg dose for registration. DESTINY-Lung02 led to FDA accelerated approval of T-DXd for HER2-mutant NSCLC, establishing T-DXd as the first approved HER2-directed therapy for lung cancer and expanding the ADC paradigm beyond breast and gastric cancers into thoracic oncology.

title: Tarlatamab versus Standard Chemotherapy in Previously Treated Small-Cell Lung Cancer (DeLLphi-301 Updated Analysis)
text_chunk: The updated analysis of DeLLphi-301, presented with extended follow-up, provided mature data on tarlatamab in previously treated extensive-stage SCLC. At the recommended 10mg dose with median follow-up of 17.5 months, confirmed ORR was 40% with median DOR of 9.7 months. Median PFS was 4.9 months and median OS was 14.3 months. The 12-month OS rate was 57%, a landmark in relapsed SCLC where historical 12-month OS rates with topotecan or lurbinectedin are approximately 30-35%. Subgroup analyses showed consistent benefit regardless of prior platinum sensitivity, number of prior lines, and brain metastasis status (though patients with untreated brain metastases were excluded). Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) was largely confined to the first two treatment cycles with grade 3+ CRS in only 1%. ICANS occurred in 10% of patients (grade 3+ in 3%). The favorable risk-benefit profile led to ongoing phase 3 investigation of tarlatamab versus standard chemotherapy (DeLLphi-304). This data further established the DLL3-targeting BiTE platform as a promising new therapeutic class for SCLC.
